original_thesis: Expert-to-novice knowledge transmission. Curse-of-knowledge is the core problem (experts forget what it's like not to know); scaffolding provides temporary support; Feynman technique forces the expert to rebuild from the learner's level; boundaries define what's in/out of scope; cognitive-load theory explains capacity limits; expertise-reversal warns when scaffolding itself becomes overhead for advanced learners.
corrected_thesis: Expert-to-novice knowledge transmission. Curse-of-knowledge is the core problem (experts forget what it's like not to know); Feynman technique forces the expert to rebuild from the learner's level; cognitive-load theory explains working memory limits; checklists formalize what must be transferred; expertise-reversal-effect warns when teaching methods that help novices actually hurt experts; lock-in warns when transferred knowledge creates dependency on a specific framework rather than genuine understanding.
what_this_stack_defeats: Expert blind spots, failed onboarding, information dumps, framework dependency
notes: Original thesis mentioned boundaries and scaffolding — not members. Lock-in as a member is the weakest link but defensible: knowledge transfer that creates lock-in to a specific mental model is a real failure mode.
